annotations.label: Natural number

anchor.snippet: the natural numbers are the numbers

annotations.note: The type `Nat` (notation `ℕ`) is the core formalization of the natural numbers, with `ℕ` notation defined in Mathlib.Data.Nat.Notation.

annotations.label: Cardinal number

anchor.snippet: they are called cardinal numbers

annotations.note: `Cardinal` is defined as the quotient of types under equinumerosity, exactly the cardinal-number notion.

annotations.label: Ordinal number

anchor.snippet: they are called ordinal numbers

annotations.note: `Ordinal` is defined as the quotient of well-orders by order isomorphism, the standard order-type/ordinal notion.

annotations.label: Nominal number

anchor.snippet: referred to as nominal numbers

annotations.note: A nominal number is a non-mathematical labeling use of numerals and has no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Cardinality

anchor.snippet: the size of a collection , is called cardinality

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mk α` (notation `#α`) is the cardinality of a type, exactly the size-of-a-collection notion.

annotations.label: Equal cardinality via one-to-one correspondence

anchor.snippet: have the same size or cardinality if they have a one-to-one correspondence

annotations.note: `Cardinal.eq : #α = #β ↔ Nonempty (α ≃ β)` states that equal cardinality is equivalent to existence of a bijection.

annotations.label: Size relationship between collections

anchor.snippet: this can be used to define a size relationship between them

annotations.note: `Cardinal.le_def : #α ≤ #β ↔ Nonempty (α ↪ β)` defines the size relationship via existence of an injection.

annotations.label: Sequence

anchor.snippet: a sequence is a function that assigns an object to each position

annotations.note: `Stream'.Seq α` formalizes (possibly infinite) sequences indexed by positions, generalizing the function-on-positions notion.

annotations.label: Order type

anchor.snippet: Every well-ordered set has an order type

annotations.note: `Ordinal.type r` assigns to each well-order its ordinal, exactly the order-type notion.

annotations.label: Natural numbers as simplest infinite well-ordered set

anchor.snippet: they form the simplest infinite well-ordered set, with order type ω

annotations.note: `Ordinal.type_nat_lt : typeLT ℕ = ω` states the order type of `ℕ` under `<` is `ω` (`Ordinal.omega0`).

annotations.label: Two definitions of natural numbers

anchor.snippet: The term natural numbers has two common definitions

annotations.note: This is a terminological/meta remark about conventions (including vs excluding 0), not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Positive integers and non-negative integers

anchor.snippet: are often called the positive integers and the non-negative integers

annotations.note: `PNat` (notation `ℕ+`) formalizes the positive integers, while non-negative integers are `ℕ` itself.

annotations.label: Whole numbers

anchor.snippet: The phrase whole numbers is frequently used

annotations.note: This is an ambiguous terminology remark, not a distinct mathematical object to formalize.

annotations.label: Numeral

anchor.snippet: A numeral is a symbol or grouping of symbols

annotations.note: Mathlib has numeral representations like `Num`/`PosNum` and `OfNat`, but the general informal notion of a numeral-as-symbol is not directly formalized.

annotations.label: Decimal system

anchor.snippet: The decimal system which uses Arabic numerals

annotations.note: `Nat.digits b n` gives the base-`b` digit representation, with the decimal system being the `b = 10` instance.

annotations.label: Counting

anchor.snippet: Counting is the process of iterating through the natural numbers

annotations.note: Counting as a cognitive/procedural process is not formalized, though `List.range` enumerates naturals.

annotations.label: Cardinality principle

anchor.snippet: The cardinality principle is the understanding

annotations.note: The cardinality principle is a cognitive-developmental concept with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Successor

anchor.snippet: another unique natural number that it is followed by

annotations.note: `Nat.succ` is the successor constructor of `Nat` (defined in Lean core and used throughout Mathlib).

annotations.label: Peano axioms

anchor.snippet: The five Peano axioms are

annotations.note: `Nat` satisfies the Peano axioms (zero, injective successor, recursor `Nat.rec`), but Mathlib has no single bundled `PeanoAxioms` structure.

annotations.label: Axiom of induction

anchor.snippet: The axiom of induction

annotations.note: The induction/recursion principle `Nat.rec` is the core recursor, with variants like `Nat.le_induction` and `Nat.strong_induction_on` in Mathlib.

annotations.label: von Neumann ordinals construction

anchor.snippet: the standard solution (due to John von Neumann )

annotations.note: `PSet.ofNat n` constructs the n-th von Neumann ordinal (with `PSet.omega` for ω) in the ZFC set-theory development.

annotations.label: Set-theoretic cardinality

anchor.snippet: a set S has n elements if there is a one-to-one correspondence

annotations.note: `Nat.card`/`Fintype.card` give the number of elements of a type, characterized via bijections (e.g. `Fintype.card_eq`).

annotations.label: Set-theoretic order

anchor.snippet: n ≤ m if and only if n is a subset of m

annotations.note: Von Neumann ordinals and their membership/subset order exist in `SetTheory.ZFC.Ordinal`, but the specific `n ≤ m ↔ n ⊆ m` characterization for `ℕ` is not stated (Mathlib's `ℕ` order is the core `<`).

annotations.label: Zermelo ordinals

anchor.snippet: Another construction sometimes called Zermelo ordinals

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the von Neumann construction (`PSet.ofNat`), not the Zermelo singleton-nesting construction.

annotations.label: Definition of addition

anchor.snippet: sending each natural number to the next one

annotations.note: `Nat.add` is the recursively-defined addition on `Nat` (Lean core), with `Nat.add_zero`/`Nat.add_succ` giving its defining equations.

annotations.label: Addition as repeated application of successor

anchor.snippet: addition can be seen as repeated application of the successor function

annotations.note: `Nat.add_succ : a + succ b = succ (a + b)` (with `Nat.succ_eq_add_one`) realizes addition as repeated successor application.

annotations.label: (ℕ, +) is a commutative monoid

anchor.snippet: is a commutative monoid with identity element 0

annotations.note: `Nat.instAddCommMonoid` provides the additive commutative monoid structure on `ℕ` with identity `0`.

annotations.label: (ℕ, +) is a free monoid on one generator

anchor.snippet: It is a free monoid on one generator

annotations.note: `FreeMonoid`/`FreeAddMonoid` are formalized, but the isomorphism `ℕ ≃ FreeAddMonoid Unit` (free on one generator) is not a named declaration.

annotations.label: Cancellation property and embedding in a group

anchor.snippet: satisfies the cancellation property

annotations.note: `Nat.instAddCancelCommMonoid` gives the additive cancellation property, and `ℕ` embeds into the group `ℤ` via `Nat.castRingHom`.

annotations.label: Smallest group containing ℕ is the integers

anchor.snippet: The smallest group containing the natural numbers is the integers

annotations.note: The integers `Int` and the embedding `Nat.cast : ℕ → ℤ` exist, but the universal Grothendieck-group/group-completion property of `ℕ` is not formalized as a theorem.

annotations.label: Definition of multiplication

anchor.snippet: given that addition has been defined

annotations.note: `Nat.mul` is the recursively-defined multiplication on `Nat` (Lean core), built from addition.

annotations.label: (ℕ, ×) is a free commutative monoid

anchor.snippet: into a free commutative monoid with identity element 1

annotations.note: `Nat.factorizationEquiv : ℕ+ ≃ {f : ℕ →₀ ℕ // ∀ p ∈ f.support, Prime p}` exhibits the multiplicative monoid `ℕ+` as the free commutative monoid on the primes.

annotations.label: Primes generate the multiplicative monoid

anchor.snippet: a generator set for this monoid is the set of prime numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.factorization_prod_pow_eq_self` expresses each positive natural as a product of prime powers, so primes generate the multiplicative monoid.

annotations.label: Distributive law

anchor.snippet: which is expressed in the distribution law

annotations.note: `Nat.left_distrib` (and `Nat.right_distrib`) state `a * (b + c) = a*b + a*c`, the distributive law on `ℕ`.

annotations.label: ℕ not closed under subtraction

anchor.snippet: is not closed under subtraction

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes truncated subtraction `Nat.sub` (with `Nat.sub_eq_zero_of_le`), reflecting that ordinary subtraction is not total on `ℕ`, but non-closure is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: ℕ is a semiring, not a ring

anchor.snippet: instead it is a semiring

annotations.note: `Nat.instCommSemiring` gives `ℕ` its commutative semiring structure (it cannot be a ring as it lacks additive inverses).

annotations.label: ℕ excluding 0 has no identity element

anchor.snippet: has no identity element

annotations.note: The claim that the positive naturals under addition form a semigroup without identity is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Total order on natural numbers

anchor.snippet: A total order on the natural numbers is defined by letting

annotations.note: `Nat.instLinearOrder` provides the total (linear) order on `ℕ`.

annotations.label: Order compatible with operations

anchor.snippet: This order is compatible with the arithmetical operations

annotations.note: `Nat.instIsStrictOrderedRing` (and `Nat.instIsOrderedAddMonoid`) establish that the order is compatible with addition and multiplication.

annotations.label: Well-ordering of the natural numbers

anchor.snippet: they are well-ordered : every non-empty set of natural numbers has a least element

annotations.note: `Nat.find` constructs the least natural satisfying a decidable predicate, and the `WellFoundedLT ℕ`/`IsWellOrder ℕ (·<·)` instances express the well-ordering principle.

annotations.label: Euclidean division

anchor.snippet: for any two natural numbers a and b with b ≠ 0 there are natural numbers q and r

annotations.note: `Nat.div_add_mod : b * (a / b) + a % b = a` with `Nat.mod_lt` gives existence of quotient and remainder with `r < b`.

annotations.label: Quotient and remainder

anchor.snippet: The number q is called the quotient and r is called the remainder

annotations.note: `Nat.div` (quotient, notation `/`) and `Nat.mod` (remainder, notation `%`) are the core division operations on `ℕ`.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of quotient and remainder

anchor.snippet: The numbers q and r are uniquely determined by a and b

annotations.note: `Nat.div_mod_unique` characterizes the quotient and remainder uniquely given `r < b`.

annotations.label: Closure under addition and multiplication

anchor.section: Algebraic properties satisfied by the natural numbers
anchor.snippet: Closure under addition and multiplication

annotations.note: Closure is inherent in the total operations `Nat.add`/`Nat.mul : ℕ → ℕ → ℕ`, packaged in `Nat.instCommSemiring`.

annotations.label: Associativity

anchor.section: Algebraic properties satisfied by the natural numbers
anchor.snippet: Associativity : for all natural numbers a , b , and c

annotations.note: `Nat.add_assoc` and `Nat.mul_assoc` state associativity of addition and multiplication on `ℕ`.

annotations.label: Commutativity

anchor.section: Algebraic properties satisfied by the natural numbers
anchor.snippet: Commutativity : for all natural numbers a and b

annotations.note: `Nat.add_comm` and `Nat.mul_comm` state commutativity of addition and multiplication on `ℕ`.

annotations.label: Existence of identity elements

anchor.section: Algebraic properties satisfied by the natural numbers
anchor.snippet: Existence of identity elements : for every natural number a

annotations.note: `Nat.add_zero`/`Nat.zero_add` (identity 0) and `Nat.mul_one`/`Nat.one_mul` (identity 1) provide the identity elements.

annotations.label: Distributivity of multiplication over addition

anchor.section: Algebraic properties satisfied by the natural numbers
anchor.snippet: Distributivity of multiplication over addition

annotations.note: `Nat.left_distrib`/`Nat.right_distrib` express distributivity of multiplication over addition on `ℕ`.

annotations.label: No nonzero zero divisors

anchor.section: Algebraic properties satisfied by the natural numbers
anchor.snippet: No nonzero zero divisors

annotations.note: `Nat.mul_eq_zero : m * n = 0 ↔ m = 0 ∨ n = 0` shows `ℕ` has no nonzero zero divisors (also via `Nat.instCommMonoidWithZero`).

annotations.label: Construction of the integers

anchor.snippet: the result is the integers , which include zero and negative numbers

annotations.note: `Int` (notation `ℤ`) formalizes the integers including zero and negatives (defined in Lean core, ring structure in Mathlib).

annotations.label: Construction of the rational numbers

anchor.snippet: the result is the rational numbers , including fractions

annotations.note: `Rat` (notation `ℚ`) formalizes the rationals as reduced fractions (core type, field structure developed in Mathlib).

annotations.label: Construction of the real numbers

anchor.snippet: If every infinite decimal is considered to be a number, the result is the real numbers

annotations.note: `Real` (notation `ℝ`) is constructed as the Cauchy completion of `ℚ` (`structure Real where ofCauchy`).

annotations.label: Construction of the complex numbers

anchor.snippet: If every solution of a polynomial equation is considered to be a number, the result is the complex numbers

annotations.note: `Complex` (notation `ℂ`) is defined as pairs of reals (real and imaginary parts), the standard complex-number construction.
